sections.plain_summary: Depression is a mental health condition where persistent sadness, loss of interest in activities, and negative thoughts interfere with daily life. It's treatable through therapy, medication, lifestyle changes, or a combination of these approaches.
sections.detailed_summary: Depression (also called major depressive disorder or clinical depression) is characterized by a sustained period of low mood, reduced ability to experience pleasure, changes in sleep and appetite, fatigue, difficulty concentrating, and sometimes thoughts of worthlessness or self-harm. Unlike temporary sadness, clinical depression lasts at least two weeks and significantly impairs functioning at work, school, or in relationships. It involves changes in brain chemistry, particularly affecting neurotransmitters like serotonin, norepinephrine, and dopamine. Depression is highly treatable and varies widely in severity and presentation between individuals.

prevalence.summary: About 1 in 5 adults experience depression in their lifetime.
prevalence.estimate: 5–10% of adults experience depression in any given year; lifetime prevalence around 15–20%
prevalence.notes: Rates are higher in women than men; may be underdiagnosed in men due to different symptom presentation. Varies by country, socioeconomic status, and access to healthcare. Rates increased during the COVID-19 pandemic.

subgroups.name: Major Depressive Disorder (MDD)
subgroups.description: The most common form, with severe depressive episodes lasting at least two weeks that impair functioning.
distinguishing_features: Requires five or more symptoms present nearly every day for at least two weeks
Symptoms cause significant distress or functional impairment
Not attributable to another medical or psychiatric condition

subgroups.name: Persistent Depressive Disorder (Dysthymia)
subgroups.description: Chronic, less severe depression lasting at least two years in adults (one year in children), with symptoms that come and go but never fully resolve.
distinguishing_features: Lower intensity than MDD but longer duration
Periods of slightly better mood may occur but do not last long
Often called 'depression with a smile' because people may continue functioning while feeling persistently low

subgroups.name: Seasonal Affective Disorder (SAD)
subgroups.description: Depression linked to seasonal changes, typically occurring in fall and winter when daylight decreases.
distinguishing_features: Symptoms improve with increased light exposure or spring arrival
More common in higher latitudes and in people with light sensitivity

subgroups.name: Postpartum Depression
subgroups.description: Depression occurring after childbirth, distinct from 'baby blues' which resolve within two weeks.
distinguishing_features: Onset within weeks to months after delivery
May include thoughts of harming self or infant
Relates to hormonal shifts, sleep deprivation, and life stress

subgroups.name: Depression with Atypical Features
subgroups.description: Depression characterized by mood reactivity (mood brightens in response to positive events) alongside other symptoms.
distinguishing_features: Increased appetite or weight gain (opposite of typical depression)

subgroups.name: Treatment-Resistant Depression (TRD)
subgroups.description: Depression that does not respond adequately to two or more antidepressant medications at therapeutic doses.
distinguishing_features: Persistent symptoms despite standard medication trials
May respond to alternative treatments like ketamine, TMS, or ECT

symptoms.notes: Present nearly every day for most of the day; may be described as feeling numb rather than actively sad

symptoms.symptom: Loss of interest or pleasure in hobbies and activities (anhedonia)
symptoms.notes: Previously enjoyed activities feel pointless; includes reduced interest in social engagement

symptoms.symptom: Significant weight change or appetite change
symptoms.notes: May increase or decrease; not due to dieting or intentional weight loss

symptoms.symptom: Insomnia or hypersomnia (sleeping too little or too much)
symptoms.notes: Early morning awakening is common; sleep does not feel restorative
Present even after adequate rest; simple tasks feel exhausting

symptoms.symptom: Difficulty concentrating, remembering, or making decisions
symptoms.notes: May notice reduced work or academic performance; mind feels foggy or slow
Others may notice slowed speech, movement, or restlessness; observable to others, not just subjective

symptoms.symptom: Feelings of worthlessness or excessive guilt
symptoms.notes: Guilt often about past events or perceived failures; self-criticism is harsh and persistent

symptoms.symptom: Recurrent thoughts of death, dying, or self-harm
symptoms.notes: Ranges from passive death wishes to active suicidal ideation with or without a plan; requires urgent evaluation
May be more prominent than sadness, especially in men and adolescents; oversensitivity to criticism
Avoid friends, family, or social events; feel burden to others
Difficulty starting tasks; procrastination; neglect of self-care or hygiene

key_patterns: A sustained period (two weeks or longer) of low mood or loss of interest that is different from your baseline
Symptoms are present most days and interfere with work, school, relationships, or self-care
The symptoms are not better explained by another condition (thyroid disorder, grief, substance use, etc.)
You have noticed changes in sleep, appetite, energy, or ability to concentrate alongside low mood
Negative thoughts about yourself, the future, or the world feel persistent and difficult to challenge

at_home.name: PHQ-9 (Patient Health Questionnaire-9)
at_home.description: A nine-question self-report screening tool where you rate how often you've experienced depressive symptoms over the past two weeks (0=not at all, 3=nearly every day). Scores range 0–27; scores ≥10 suggest possible depression. Available free online. Useful to track changes over time.
at_home.safety_notes: This is a screening tool, not a diagnosis. High scores warrant professional evaluation. If you answer '3' (nearly every day) for item 9 about self-harm, seek professional help immediately.

at_home.name: Mood tracking journal
at_home.description: Daily record of mood (0–10 scale), energy, sleep, and what happened that day. Over 2–4 weeks, patterns emerge: seasonal triggers, days better/worse, link between events and mood. Use a simple app or paper diary.
at_home.safety_notes: Journaling can feel overwhelming during severe depression. Start small (one sentence per day) if needed. Should not replace professional care.

clinical.name: Structured clinical interview (SCID or similar)
clinical.description: Clinician-administered diagnostic interview that systematically assesses depression diagnostic criteria, duration, severity, and rule-out other conditions. Gold standard for diagnosis.
clinical.availability: Performed by psychiatrist, psychologist, or trained mental health clinician during initial assessment.

clinical.name: Hamilton Depression Rating Scale (HAM-D) or Montgomery-Åsberg Depression Rating Scale (MADRS)
clinical.description: Clinician-administered severity rating scales with 17–21 items assessing mood, sleep, guilt, suicidality, etc. Used to track treatment response over time. More detailed than PHQ-9.
clinical.availability: Administered by mental health professionals; typically used in clinical trials or specialized clinics.

clinical.name: Thyroid function tests (TSH, free T4)
clinical.description: Blood tests to rule out hypothyroidism, which can mimic or contribute to depression. Essential first step because thyroid disorder is treatable and overlaps symptomatically with depression.
clinical.availability: Ordered by primary care doctor or psychiatrist; routine lab test at any clinical facility.

clinical.name: Complete metabolic panel and CBC (complete blood count)
clinical.description: Blood tests to check for anemia, electrolyte imbalances, liver or kidney dysfunction, and vitamin deficiencies (B12, folate) that can contribute to depression.
clinical.availability: Ordered by primary care doctor; routine lab test.

clinical.name: Vitamin D and B12 levels
clinical.description: Deficiency in vitamin D (especially relevant in SAD) or B12 can worsen depression or contribute to depressive symptoms. Blood draw required.
clinical.availability: Can be ordered as part of metabolic panel or separately by any clinician.

clinical.name: Psychosocial assessment
clinical.description: Qualitative interview by clinician exploring personal history, life stressors, trauma, substance use, social support, and functional impairment. Informs treatment planning.
clinical.availability: Conducted by psychiatrist, psychologist, or therapist during intake.

clinical.name: Brain-derived neurotrophic factor (BDNF) or inflammatory markers (CRP, IL-6)
clinical.description: Research-stage biomarkers that may correlate with depression severity or response to treatment. Not yet standard clinical practice. May help identify subtypes or predict treatment response.
clinical.availability: Available in research settings and some specialized clinics; not routine clinical care.

clinical.name: Neuroimaging (fMRI, PET scan)
clinical.description: Brain imaging to visualize activity, metabolism, or structure. Useful for research and some treatment-resistant cases but not routinely diagnostic.
clinical.availability: Specialized research centers or academic medical centers; expensive and not typically covered by insurance for routine depression.

related_conditions.name: Anxiety disorders (generalized anxiety, social anxiety, panic disorder)
related_conditions.explanation: Depression and anxiety frequently co-occur; they share overlapping neurobiological pathways and risk factors. Many individuals experience both simultaneously.

related_conditions.name: Bipolar disorder
related_conditions.explanation: Bipolar disorder includes depressive episodes but is distinguished by episodes of mania or hypomania. Misdiagnosis of bipolar as unipolar depression can lead to inappropriate antidepressant use without mood stabilizer.

related_conditions.name: Persistent grief or bereavement
related_conditions.explanation: Normal grief from loss can resemble depression, but typically mood gradually improves and preserved ability to experience joy in others' company. Complicated grief that is severe and persistent may warrant depression diagnosis.

related_conditions.name: Hypothyroidism
related_conditions.explanation: Thyroid hormone is essential for neurotransmitter function and mood regulation. Hypothyroidism can cause or worsen depressive symptoms and must be ruled out before antidepressant trials.

related_conditions.name: Vitamin D or B12 deficiency
related_conditions.explanation: Both vitamins are essential for neurological function and mood regulation. Deficiency is associated with depressive symptoms and can be corrected with supplementation.

related_conditions.name: Sleep disorders (insomnia, sleep apnea)
related_conditions.explanation: Sleep disruption is both a symptom of depression and a risk factor that worsens mood and cognition. Treating underlying sleep disorder often improves depression outcomes.

related_conditions.name: Chronic pain conditions
related_conditions.explanation: Depression and chronic pain share overlapping neural pathways and genetic factors; each can exacerbate the other. Treatment often requires addressing both.

related_conditions.name: Substance use disorder
related_conditions.explanation: Substances like alcohol or cannabis may temporarily ease depressive symptoms but worsen underlying depression over time. Untreated depression is a risk factor for substance misuse.

related_conditions.name: ADHD
related_conditions.explanation: ADHD and depression often co-occur. ADHD-related executive dysfunction and failure can trigger depression, while depression can worsen ADHD symptoms.

related_conditions.name: Complex PTSD or trauma-related disorders
related_conditions.explanation: Trauma and chronic stress alter the brain regions and neurotransmitter systems involved in mood regulation, increasing depression risk. Trauma-informed treatment is often necessary.

strategies.name: Psychotherapy (Cognitive Behavioral Therapy, CBT)
strategies.description: A structured, evidence-based approach that teaches you to identify and challenge negative thought patterns and develop coping strategies. Typically 12–20 sessions. Helps break the cycle of negative thinking and avoidance.
strategies.notes: Effective for mild to moderate depression and as maintenance after recovery. Works better if you're willing to practice skills between sessions. May take 4–8 weeks to notice benefit.

strategies.name: Psychotherapy (Interpersonal Therapy, IPT)
strategies.description: Focuses on relationships, life roles, and communication patterns. Helps identify how interpersonal conflicts or role changes contribute to depression and develop better coping strategies.
strategies.notes: Particularly useful if depression is linked to relationship problems, grief, or major life transitions. Similar timeline and effectiveness to CBT.

strategies.name: Psychotherapy (Psychodynamic or exploratory therapy)
strategies.description: Explores how past experiences, unconscious patterns, and emotions shape current mood and behavior. Longer-term approach (6 months to years) than CBT or IPT.
strategies.notes: Helpful for understanding root causes and patterns; less directive than CBT. May work well in combination with medication.

strategies.name: Selective Serotonin Reuptake Inhibitors (SSRIs)
strategies.description: First-line antidepressants (e.g., sertraline, citalopram, escitalopram) that increase available serotonin by blocking reuptake. Typical dose takes 4–8 weeks to show full effect. Effectiveness varies by individual.
strategies.notes: Takes trial-and-error to find the right medication and dose. Common side effects include nausea, sexual dysfunction, or sleep changes (usually temporary). Do not stop abruptly; taper under medical supervision.

strategies.name: Serotonin-Norepinephrine Reuptake Inhibitors (SNRIs)
strategies.description: Second-line option (e.g., venlafaxine, duloxetine) that affects both serotonin and norepinephrine. Similar timeline and effectiveness to SSRIs; may work better if SSRIs fail.
strategies.notes: Same caveats about timing and side effects as SSRIs. May have slightly higher withdrawal potential.

strategies.name: Tricyclic antidepressants (TCAs) or other older classes
strategies.description: Older antidepressants (e.g., amitriptyline) less commonly used as first-line due to side effects, but may be effective for treatment-resistant cases or co-occurring pain.
strategies.notes: More side effects (sedation, weight gain, dry mouth) than SSRIs. Require heart monitoring. Useful if SSRIs/SNRIs fail.

strategies.name: Atypical antipsychotics (adjunctive)
strategies.description: Medications like aripiprazole or quetiapine added to an antidepressant when SSRI/SNRI alone is insufficient. Particularly useful in treatment-resistant depression.
strategies.notes: Side effects include weight gain, metabolic effects, sedation. Requires monitoring. Reserved for moderate to severe depression not responding to standard agents.

strategies.name: Ketamine or esketamine (Spravato)
strategies.description: Rapid-acting antidepressants (work in hours to days rather than weeks) for severe or treatment-resistant depression, especially with suicidal ideation. Nasal spray or IV infusion.
strategies.notes: Emerging treatment with growing evidence. Requires clinical setting for monitoring. Disassociation or euphoria possible. Often used alongside standard antidepressants.

strategies.name: Transcranial Magnetic Stimulation (TMS) or Deep TMS
strategies.description: Non-invasive brain stimulation using magnetic pulses to activate regions involved in mood. Typically 5 sessions per week for 4–6 weeks. Effective for treatment-resistant depression.
strategies.notes: Well-tolerated; mild headache or scalp discomfort possible. Requires outpatient visits. FDA-approved for treatment-resistant depression. Cost varies.

strategies.name: Electroconvulsive Therapy (ECT)
strategies.description: Under general anesthesia, controlled electrical current induces a brief seizure to reset brain activity. For severe, psychotic, or treatment-resistant depression with suicidality. 6–12 sessions over 2–4 weeks.
strategies.notes: Most effective treatment for severe depression but carries risk of memory loss or cognitive effects. Requires hospital setting. Reserved for severe cases when other options fail.

strategies.name: Aerobic exercise
strategies.description: Regular physical activity (150 min/week moderate intensity or 75 min/week vigorous) reduces depression severity, boosts mood via endorphins and neurotrophic factors. Walking, jogging, cycling, swimming all effective.
strategies.notes: Effect size comparable to medication in some studies. Benefit takes 2–4 weeks to emerge. Requires consistency. Structured exercise (class, trainer) increases adherence for some.

strategies.name: Light therapy (phototherapy)
strategies.description: Exposure to a bright light box (10,000 lux) for 20–30 minutes each morning, especially for seasonal affective disorder. Mechanism involves circadian rhythm regulation and serotonin production.
strategies.notes: Most effective for SAD but may help non-seasonal depression too. Onset 3–7 days. Use within 1–2 hours of waking. Avoid if taking photosensitizing medications.

strategies.name: Sleep hygiene optimization
strategies.description: Consistent sleep schedule, dark/cool bedroom, limit screens before bed, avoid caffeine/alcohol, establish wind-down routine. Improved sleep quality can ease depressive symptoms.
strategies.notes: Often depression makes sleep improvement difficult; may need treatment for insomnia alongside depression therapy. Relaxation techniques (progressive muscle relaxation, breathing) help.

strategies.name: Cognitive restructuring or thought records
strategies.description: Practicing CBT skills outside therapy: record negative automatic thoughts, examine evidence, develop balanced alternative thoughts. Strengthens ability to challenge depressive thinking.
strategies.notes: Requires practice and patience. Apps like MindShift or Thought Diary can help structure this. Works best when combined with professional therapy.

strategies.name: Behavioral activation
strategies.description: Gradually increasing engagement in meaningful activities and time outside, even if you don't feel like it. Avoidance and isolation perpetuate depression; activity breaks the cycle.
strategies.notes: Start small (10-minute walk, call a friend) and build. Mood often follows action rather than preceding it. Scheduling helps. One of the most effective self-help tools.

strategies.name: Social connection and support
strategies.description: Regular contact with trusted friends, family, or groups; reduce isolation. Social engagement activates reward circuits and provides practical and emotional support.
strategies.notes: Depression makes reaching out feel impossible; start with low-pressure contact (text, call). Online communities or support groups count. Loneliness worsens depression.

strategies.name: Mindfulness meditation or acceptance-based therapy
strategies.description: Practicing non-judgmental awareness of thoughts and emotions without trying to change them. Reduces rumination and increases psychological flexibility. Apps like Headspace or Insight Timer offer guided practices.
strategies.notes: Takes practice; not immediately intuitive for everyone. Can feel unhelpful during severe depression. Best as adjunct to other treatments. 10–20 minutes daily most effective.

strategies.name: Vitamin D supplementation
strategies.description: If deficient, supplementation (1000–4000 IU daily depending on level and sun exposure) can improve mood, especially in winter or if you are indoors frequently. Check levels first.
strategies.notes: Only beneficial if you're deficient. Not effective for non-deficient individuals. Takes 4–8 weeks to see benefit. Combine with light exposure for SAD.

strategies.name: Omega-3 fatty acids (fish oil)
strategies.description: Eicosapentaenoic acid (EPA)-rich fish oil supplements may have modest antidepressant effects, possibly via reduction of inflammation and support of neuronal function.
strategies.notes: Evidence is mixed. Dose typically 1–2g EPA/day. Takes 4–8 weeks. Fish oil can interact with blood thinners. Use only as adjunct to primary treatments.

strategies.name: Folate (B9) and B12 supplementation
strategies.description: If deficient, supplementation improves mood and cognitive function. Particularly relevant if you have anemia, pernicious anemia, or poor diet.
strategies.notes: Only effective if deficient; check levels first. Supplementation can take weeks to months. Some people require B12 injections for absorption.

strategies.name: Reducing alcohol and avoiding recreational drugs
strategies.description: Alcohol is a CNS depressant and worsens mood over time, especially with regular use. Cannabis, stimulants, and other drugs can worsen depression or delay recovery.
strategies.notes: Temporary relief from substances leads to worse depression rebound. If substance use is part of your presentation, addiction treatment may be necessary first.

strategies.name: Structured routine and goal-setting
strategies.description: Establish a predictable daily routine (wake time, meals, bedtime) and set small, achievable goals. Structure counteracts the paralysis and purposelessness of depression.
strategies.notes: Start with tiny habits (brush teeth, one 10-minute task). Progress gradually. Avoiding perfectionism helps—'good enough' counts.

strategies.name: Valued living or meaning-making
strategies.description: Identify what matters to you (relationships, creativity, helping others, learning) and take small steps toward those values, even if depressed. Reconnecting with meaning aids recovery.
strategies.notes: Requires reflection. Therapy can help clarify values. Pursuing values despite low mood is powerful; mood often follows.

things_to_avoid: Alcohol and recreational drugs — they worsen depression and delay recovery.
Isolation and withdrawal from others — loneliness amplifies depressive thinking and removes support.
Rumination and dwelling on negative thoughts — feeds the depression cycle; redirect to present-moment or action.
Sleep deprivation — poor sleep worsens mood and cognitive symptoms dramatically.
Caffeine excess — can increase anxiety and worsen sleep, which exacerbates depression.
Skipping meals or poor nutrition — unstable blood sugar and nutrient deficiency worsen mood and energy.
Comparing yourself to others on social media — triggers worthlessness and reduces motivation.
Putting off seeking help or waiting for depression to 'go away on its own' — early treatment is more effective.
Abruptly stopping antidepressants without medical guidance — causes withdrawal symptoms and rebound depression.
Over-responsibility for others' emotions or self-blame — increases guilt and hopelessness.
Excess time in dark or confined spaces — impacts mood and circadian rhythm negatively.

key_levers.lever: Regular aerobic exercise (150 min/week at moderate intensity)
key_levers.why_it_matters: Exercise increases brain-derived neurotrophic factor (BDNF), boosts serotonin and endorphins, improves sleep, and reduces inflammation. Effect size equals medication in some studies.
key_levers.how_to_start: Choose an activity you tolerate (walking counts). Commit to three 20-minute sessions per week. Use a calendar or app to track. Partner with a friend for accountability.
key_levers.timeline: 2–4 weeks to notice mood shift; 8–12 weeks for full benefit.

key_levers.why_it_matters: Therapy directly addresses the cognitive and behavioral patterns maintaining depression. Skills learned persist after therapy ends. Combination with medication is more effective than either alone.
key_levers.how_to_start: Find a therapist (psychiatrist, psychologist, licensed counselor) who specializes in depression and offers CBT or IPT. Most offer 30–50-minute weekly sessions. Start with intake to assess fit.
key_levers.timeline: 6–8 weeks to initial benefit; 12–16 weeks for meaningful change. Maintenance benefits continue long-term.

key_levers.lever: Medication optimization (SSRI, SNRI, or other antidepressant as appropriate)
key_levers.why_it_matters: Restores neurochemical balance (serotonin, norepinephrine) that underlies depression. Allows energy and motivation to reengage with therapy and lifestyle changes. Prevents relapse.
key_levers.how_to_start: Consult a psychiatrist or your primary care doctor. Expect trial-and-error; first medication works 60% of the time. Typical trial is 4–6 weeks per medication at therapeutic dose.
key_levers.timeline: 4–8 weeks to initial response; 8–12 weeks for full effect. May need dose adjustment or medication change.

key_levers.lever: Consistent sleep schedule and sleep hygiene
key_levers.why_it_matters: Sleep disruption is both a symptom and a driver of depression. Poor sleep worsens concentration, mood regulation, and immune function. Stabilizing sleep amplifies other treatments.
key_levers.how_to_start: Set a fixed bedtime and wake time (even weekends). Keep bedroom dark, cool, quiet. No screens 30 min before bed. Avoid caffeine after 2 PM. Use relaxation techniques (breathing, progressive muscle relaxation).
key_levers.timeline: 1–2 weeks to habit formation; 4–8 weeks to sleep quality improvement and mood lift.

key_levers.why_it_matters: Social isolation perpetuates depression; connection activates reward pathways, provides practical support, and buffers against relapse. Loneliness is a major depression trigger.
key_levers.how_to_start: Identify one person to text, call, or meet weekly. Join a group (class, support group, hobby club) to reduce pressure on single relationships. Start small (10-minute coffee).
key_levers.timeline: Immediate emotional benefit (1–2 weeks); sustained mood improvement over months as relationships deepen.

key_levers.why_it_matters: Depression paralyzes motivation; starting activities despite low mood breaks the cycle. As activity increases, mood follows. Avoidance perpetuates depression.
key_levers.how_to_start: List three small, concrete activities (not 'be happy,' but 'walk 10 min' or 'text a friend'). Schedule one per day. Do it even if you don't feel like it. Track completion.
key_levers.timeline: 1–2 weeks for increased energy; 3–4 weeks for noticeable mood shift.

key_levers.lever: Addressing co-occurring medical conditions (thyroid, vitamin deficiency, sleep apnea, pain)
key_levers.why_it_matters: These conditions mimic or worsen depression and prevent recovery. Correcting them removes obstacles to treatment and can relieve depression on their own.
key_levers.how_to_start: Order basic labs: TSH, B12, vitamin D, CBC, metabolic panel. If sleep apnea suspected, request sleep study. Address deficiencies via supplementation or medication.
key_levers.timeline: Depends on condition; typically 4–8 weeks after correction to see mood improvement.

key_levers.why_it_matters: Light regulates circadian rhythm and stimulates serotonin production. 10,000 lux for 20–30 min in morning is effective for seasonal and some non-seasonal depression.
key_levers.how_to_start: Use a light therapy box (SAD lamp) within 30 min of waking for 20–30 min, daily (or at least fall/winter). Position at eye level, 16–24 inches away.
key_levers.timeline: 3–7 days for initial effect in SAD; 2–4 weeks for full benefit. Results plateau if stopped.

common_mistakes: Waiting for motivation before taking action — depression won't lift until you act; action precedes motivation.
Trying one treatment once, then abandoning it — antidepressants take 4–8 weeks; therapy takes 6–12 weeks. Impatience leads to unnecessary switches.
Combining multiple new treatments simultaneously — can't tell what works; also overwhelming. Start 1–2, evaluate in 6–8 weeks, then adjust.
Isolating and avoiding others — feeling like a burden or unworthy causes people to withdraw; isolation deepens depression.
Relying solely on medication without therapy — medication removes symptoms faster but doesn't teach coping skills; highest relapse rates.
Relying solely on therapy without medication for severe depression — therapy alone takes longer; medication can provide enough relief to engage in therapy.
Assuming depression is permanent or that recovery is impossible — depression is highly treatable; 80%+ respond to treatment.
Setting unrealistic expectations for timeline — expecting instant relief leads to premature medication/therapy changes.
Neglecting sleep thinking 'I'll rest when I'm better' — poor sleep perpetuates depression; prioritize sleep.
Stopping medication or therapy as soon as you feel better — tapering too quickly causes relapse; continue for 6–12 months after recovery.
Trying to 'think away' depression without behavioral support — cognitive insight alone is insufficient; behavior must change (exercise, contact, activity).
Using alcohol, cannabis, or other drugs to self-treat — provides temporary relief but deepens depression and delays recovery.

specialists.role: Medical doctor who diagnoses depression, prescribes and monitors antidepressant medications, evaluates for medical causes (thyroid, etc.), and manages complex or treatment-resistant cases. Can admit to psychiatric hospital if needed.
specialists.notes: Essential if medication is part of your plan. Long wait times common; primary care doctors can initiate treatment while waiting for psychiatric appointment.

specialists.role: Delivers evidence-based psychotherapy (CBT, IPT, psychodynamic), conducts psychological testing, and co-manages treatment with psychiatrist. Cannot prescribe medication in most states (exceptions: Louisiana, New Mexico, military).
specialists.notes: Often first contact for therapy. Verify licensure and training in depression-specific modalities.

specialists.type: Licensed Professional Counselor (LPC) or Licensed Mental Health Counselor (LMHC)
specialists.role: Trained therapist who provides psychotherapy, case management, and support. Masters-level training with supervised clinical hours. Regulated by state.
specialists.notes: Accessible option; more affordable than psychologist in many areas. Verify credentials and depression experience.

specialists.role: Masters-level clinician trained in therapy, case management, and systems-level support (benefits, housing, etc.). Often skilled in trauma-informed care.
specialists.notes: Useful if depression is linked to social circumstances or trauma. May help navigate support systems.

specialists.type: Primary Care Physician (MD, DO, NP, PA)
specialists.role: Can screen for depression, rule out medical causes, prescribe and manage antidepressants, make specialist referrals. Often first contact.
specialists.notes: Variable comfort with depression treatment. Some excellent, others undertrained. Good starting point; escalate to psychiatry if medication trials fail.

specialists.type: Nurse practitioner (NP, CNP) specializing in psychiatry
specialists.role: Masters or doctoral-level nurse who can diagnose, prescribe medications, and manage depression. Often more accessible than psychiatrist; lower cost.
specialists.notes: Growing availability; often shorter wait times than psychiatrists. Verify credentials and specialization.

specialists.role: Similar role to NP: diagnosis, medication management, some clinics employ PAs for efficiency.
specialists.notes: Works under MD supervision; should have psychiatrist available for consultation.

specialists.role: Focuses on functional recovery, daily living skills, return-to-work planning, and meaningful activity engagement. Complements medical and psychotherapy treatment.
specialists.notes: Particularly useful in severe depression or after hospitalization; helps rebuild structure and purpose.

specialists.type: Peer support specialist or peer counselor
specialists.role: Individual with lived experience of mental illness who provides emotional support, navigation of services, and hope. Often in group settings or community mental health centers.
specialists.notes: Valuable for ongoing support and reducing stigma; not a replacement for professional treatment but excellent adjunct.

patient_communities.name: National Alliance on Mental Illness (NAMI)
patient_communities.notes: Peer-led support groups, educational classes (NAMI Basics, peer-to-peer), family support, and comprehensive resources. Local chapters nationwide. Helpful for families and newly diagnosed.

patient_communities.name: Depression and Bipolar Support Alliance (DBSA)
patient_communities.platform: website, online and in-person support groups
patient_communities.notes: Peer-led support groups specifically for depression (DBSA) and bipolar disorder; online and local options. Emphasis on self-management and peer support.

patient_communities.name: 7 Cups
patient_communities.notes: Free emotional support via chat with trained active listeners. Non-clinical; emphasis on listening and validation. 24/7 availability.

patient_communities.name: The Mighty
patient_communities.notes: User-generated content (stories, articles) on mental health. Normalizes depression, offers peer perspectives on treatment and coping. Engagement ranges from reading to writing.

patient_communities.name: Reddit communities: r/depression, r/mentalhealth, r/Antidepressants
patient_communities.notes: Large, active communities with diverse membership. Good for peer experience-sharing and treatment questions. Moderated but variable quality; always consult doctors.

patient_communities.name: Crisis Text Line
patient_communities.notes: Free, confidential text-based crisis support (text HOME to 741741). For suicidal ideation or acute crisis. 24/7.

patient_communities.name: National Suicide Prevention Lifeline (988)
patient_communities.notes: Trained counselors 24/7. Call or text 988 (US). For any suicidal thoughts or mental health crisis. Immediate, confidential support.

patient_communities.name: Depression and Anxiety Anonymous
patient_communities.notes: Peer-led, recovery-focused support groups. 12-step influenced but non-religious. Free.

patient_communities.name: International Association for Suicide Prevention (IASP)
patient_communities.notes: Worldwide resource for crisis lines and support by country. Useful if you're outside the US.

patient_communities.name: BumbleBee Forums (Antidepressants online community)
patient_communities.notes: Moderated forum for discussion of antidepressants, side effects, tapering, and peer support. UK-based but international members.

learning_resources.title: Feeling Good: The New Mood Therapy
learning_resources.description: Classic, accessible introduction to cognitive therapy and CBT concepts applied to depression. Includes worksheets and practical techniques for identifying and challenging distorted thoughts. Groundbreaking and practical.

learning_resources.title: The Anxiety and Phobia Workbook (and Depression companion)
learning_resources.description: Comprehensive workbook with step-by-step exercises for understanding and managing depression and anxiety. Interactive, practical, evidence-based.

learning_resources.title: Mind Over Mood: Change How You Feel by Changing the Way You Think
learning_resources.description: Practical, illustrated guide to cognitive therapy. Clear explanations of thought records and behavioral experiments. Widely recommended by therapists as self-help complement.

learning_resources.title: The Body Keeps the Score: Brain, Mind, and Body in the Healing of Trauma
learning_resources.description: In-depth exploration of how trauma affects the brain, body, and mood. Includes discussion of depression as trauma response. Dense but illuminating for understanding roots of depression.

learning_resources.title: Atomic Habits: An Easy and Proven Way to Build Good Habits and Break Bad Ones
learning_resources.description: Practical guide to habit formation through small, incremental changes. Particularly useful for behavioral activation and building routines to support depression recovery.

learning_resources.title: The Noonday Demon: An Atlas of Depression
learning_resources.description: In-depth, personal, and well-researched exploration of depression from biological, psychological, social, and philosophical angles. Long but comprehensive; combines memoir and science.

learning_resources.title: Emotional First Aid: Healing Rejection, Guilt, Failure, and Other Everyday Hurts
learning_resources.description: Practical guide to managing emotional pain and building psychological resilience. Addresses rumination, shame, and loss—common depression triggers.

learning_resources.title: NAMI Guide to Peer Support
learning_resources.description: Free, evidence-based guides on living with depression, medication, peer support, and family resources. Highly reputable, accessible, and comprehensive.

learning_resources.title: Depression and Bipolar Support Alliance (DBSA) Wellness Workbook
learning_resources.description: Peer-created workbook on self-management, medication, therapy, and coping strategies. Practical, supportive, and evidence-informed.

learning_resources.title: Indistractable: How to Control Your Attention and Choose Your Life
learning_resources.description: Practical guide to managing attention, procrastination, and distraction. Helpful for addressing executive dysfunction and inertia that accompany depression.

learning_resources.title: The Art of Happiness: A Handbook for Living
learning_resources.description: Philosophical and practical exploration of happiness, compassion, and resilience. Less clinical than CBT books but valuable for perspective and meaning-making during depression.

learning_resources.title: Why Zebras Don't Get Ulcers: Stress, Health, and Human Nature
learning_resources.description: Accessible explanation of stress physiology and its link to depression, immune function, and disease. Helps understand biological mechanisms underlying depression.

learning_resources.title: DSM-5-TR (Diagnostic and Statistical Manual) depression criteria
learning_resources.description: Official diagnostic criteria for depression. Useful for understanding how clinicians diagnose, but dense and clinical. Reference rather than self-help.

learning_resources.title: PubMed Central (NIH depression research database)
learning_resources.description: Free access to thousands of peer-reviewed depression research articles. Search for topics of interest. Technical but authoritative.

learning_resources.title: The Science of Well-Being (online course)
learning_resources.description: Free online course covering science-based strategies for happiness and mental health. Includes exercises and evidence-based techniques.

learning_resources.title: Headspace app (mindfulness and meditation)
learning_resources.description: Guided meditation and mindfulness exercises tailored to depression, sleep, and stress. Free and paid tiers. Accessible entry into meditation.

learning_resources.title: Talkspace or BetterHelp (online therapy platforms)
learning_resources.description: Licensed therapist-delivered therapy via text, phone, or video. Accessible, flexible, and often covered by insurance. Vetted providers.

learning_resources.title: Psycheducation on Depression (IOCDF resource)
learning_resources.description: Clear, evidence-based explanations of depression, treatment options, and when to seek help. Accessible language.

learning_resources.title: The Diagnostic Manual for Depression (ICD-11)
learning_resources.description: International diagnostic criteria for depression. Technical; primarily for clinicians. Reference for understanding global diagnostic standards.

sections.relevance_signal: **Gut symptoms alongside low mood, low energy, or loss of interest**. Constipation dominant. Symptoms are chronic and pervasive without clear dietary triggers.
